title: Same-sex marriages: a political challenge for Poland
content:
The issue of recognising same-sex marriages concluded abroad has turned into a political minefield in Poland, with the government lacking a clear plan on how to handle it.

“The situation is quite absurd. Poland has no real choice but to recognise marriages concluded abroad, as it has done so far. The only difference is that in this case, they involve same-sex couples,” said Bart Staszewski in an interview with FocusEurope.pl.

The dispute over the transcription of same-sex marriages in Poland intensified following landmark rulings by European and domestic courts. A key turning point was a ruling by the Court of Justice of the European Union in November 2025, which obliged member states to recognise such unions in certain situations under EU law.

Based on that judgment, the Supreme Administrative Court (NSA) ruled on 20 March 2026 that Polish authorities must transcribe foreign same-sex marriages, finding previous refusals to be in breach of, among other things, freedom of movement and th

creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: “You can’t buy time with money” – how will we spend 5% of GDP on defence?
content:
Larger ammunition stockpiles, new drone and counter-drone capabilities, rearming of the Mechanised Infantry Brigade, and much more – the Ministry of Defence (MoD) has no shortage of plans on how to spend 5% of Latvia’s gross domestic product (GDP) annually on strengthening the country’s armed forces, as provided for by a law recently adopted by the Saeima.

Does this law truly guarantee that the money will be there? Experts surveyed by Delfi admitted that it does not. Rather, it is a signal to international partners that Latvia will fulfil what has been promised to NATO and the United States. At the same time, the army has previously been “starved” for a long period, so now it must be hoped that ambitious security plans will be enabled not only by money, but also by time.

The State Defence Financing Law consists of two articles. The first defines the aim of the law – “to purposefully increase national defence capabilities through financial means”. The second specifies the schedule for increasing defence funding, expressed as a percentage of GDP.

The current law was adopted in the summer of 2014 after Russia occupied Ukrai
snippet: Larger ammunition stockpiles, new drone and counter-drone capabilities, rearming of the Mechanised Infantry Brigade, and much more – the Ministry of Defence (MoD) has no shortage of plans on how to sp
creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: What lies behind Poland’s constitutional crisis
content:
Poland’s parliament has appointed six judges to the Constitutional Tribunal (TK), but the president has sworn in only two of them. In response, parliament took matters into its own hands, and the remaining four were sworn in without the president’s participation. This is the latest chapter in Poland’s constitutional crisis.

It is hard to imagine some of the country’s most distinguished legal experts — appointed by a democratically elected parliament to serve on the highest court — spending their working days sitting in corridor armchairs or the court library instead of delivering rulings in their robes. They have no offices, no cases to hear, and the court’s president does not recognise their status.

Yet this surreal image is not the plot of a political fiction film, but the everyday reality of Poland’s Constitutional Tribunal in 2026.

For an outside observer, the situation may seem incomprehensible. To understand how this deadlock came about, one must first grasp what the Constitutional Tribunal is in Poland — and why control over it has become a political holy grail.

The Tribunal is not an ordinary court. Its m

creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: Complicated coalition talks and a scandal in the background. How did Slovenia conduct its parliamentary elections?
content:
Last month, Slovenian citizens went to the polls to elect the 90 members of the lower house of parliament.

Although no political party won a clear majority, Prime Minister Robert Golob’s liberals managed to edge slightly ahead of right-wing populist leader Janez Janša.

The elections were closely watched beyond Slovenia as well. The vote was seen as a kind of mood test among voters in the bloc, as right-wing forces increasingly gain ground in the EU’s political landscape. It was believed that Janša’s return to government would have influenced the country’s position in broader EU debates.

Who actually won?

In the vote on the 22nd of March, the main contest was between incumbent Prime Minister Robert Golob and his liberal party “Freedom Movement” (GS), and Slovenia’s three-time former prime minister Janez Janša with the right-wing Slovenian Democratic Party (SDS). Opinion polls had not predicted a clear majority for any political force in Slovenia’s 90-seat parliament.

“Freedom Movement” won 28.63% of the vote and 29 seats in pa

creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: Russian intelligence services are using increasingly dangerous methods to carry out acts of sabotage; Europe is stepping up its defences
content:
Russia’s intelligence and security services, i.e. special services, are using increasingly dangerous methods to carry out acts of sabotage and are taking less and less account of possible consequences, including indirect harm such as civilian casualties and any other collateral damage, according to the Military Intelligence and Security Service’s 2025 threat assessment and activity report.

Malicious arson as a method has been supplemented by other types of activity – physical attacks and explosions using industrially produced precursors – military explosives.

Security environment has deteriorated

Threat risks to Latvia’s defence system are closely linked to the current geopolitical situation, Russia’s full-scale war against Ukraine, and the sabotage acts, diversions against various targets and other harmful activities that took place over the past year in a number of European countries, including Latvia.

Russia has long been the most significant source of threat to Latvia and will remain so for the foreseeable future — due to its aggressive foreign policy and the development of its military capa
snippet: Russia’s intelligence and security services, i.e. special services, are using increasingly dangerous methods to carry out acts of sabotage and are taking less and less account of possible consequences
creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: “It’s probably all been sold off anyway.” Where Latvia’s gold reserves actually are
content:
The question of what happens to Latvia’s gold reserves often arises when other countries’ central banks start “moving” their gold.

It has also resurfaced now, following international media reports about the restructuring of France’s gold reserves – selling gold stored in the United States and instead acquiring an equivalent amount in Europe – which has once again brought attention to Latvia’s gold reserves.

Main points:

  • Latvia’s gold has not been sold off – it is still held and managed.
  • The Bank of Latvia still holds approximately 7.7 tonnes of gold.
  • The gold has not disappeared. The gold bars are physically stored in London, at the Bank of England.
  • There are currently no plans to increase or reduce the gold reserves.
  • The gold is not simply stored passively – it is actively managed and generates income.

A user commented on social media platform “X” under a post about France’s gold reserves: “I wonder where Latvia’s gold reserves are? I hope we haven’t got none because we’re poor… It’s probably all been sold off an
snippet: The question of what happens to Latvia’s gold reserves often arises when other countries’ central banks start “moving” their gold. It has also resurfaced now, following international media reports abo
creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: Czechia is Losing Credibility in NATO, expert says
content:
A simmering dispute between Czech President Petr Pavel and Prime Minister Andrej Babiš over who should represent the country at July’s NATO summit shows no sign of resolution.

According to expert, excluding the president could damage the Czech Republic’s credibility within the NATO – but the government appears unwilling to back down.

Czech leaders have yet to agree on who will represent the country at the NATO summit in Ankara, scheduled for July 7–8.

Recent remarks by Prime Minister Andrej Babiš suggest the issue may not be settled until at least mid-May.

Tensions resurfaced after President Petr Pavel sent a letter to the prime minister in early April, stating his intention to attend the summit as head of the Czech delegation.

The move was met with resistance from both Babiš and Foreign Minister Petr Macinka (Motorists). The prime minister argued there was no reason for the president’s participation, insisting that defence policy falls under the government’s authority rather than the presidential office.

Yet

creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: EU Climate Tool or “Brussels Scam”? Understanding the ETS
content:
The EU Emissions Trading System (ETS) has been widely debated in Poland for years, with its critics arguing that it is ineffective, harms the economy, and channels revenues from emissions trading into the EU budget. 

These claims, however, are oversimplifications that are not supported by the facts. So what is the reality behind the ETS?

In recent months, the EU Emissions Trading System has become one of the most contentious topics in public debate — not only in Poland, but across the European Union — amid planned reforms and the rising costs of the energy transition.

In Poland, the system has sparked particularly strong controversy, especially on the right of the political spectrum. Przemysław Czarnek, a Law and Justice (PiS) MP and the party’s candidate for prime minister, recently argued that
snippet: The EU Emissions Trading System (ETS) has been widely debated in Poland for years, with its critics arguing that it is ineffective, harms the economy, and channels revenues from emissions trading into
creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: A Pro-Russian Turn or Systemic Change? Bulgaria Faces a Defining Choice
content:
The parliamentary elections in Bulgaria delivered a result whose scale surprised even the most seasoned observers of the political scene in Sofia.

Rumen Radev and his movement Progressive Bulgaria secured a parliamentary majority, signaling the end of the long-standing dominance of the centre-right GERB party led by Boyko Borisov.

“Radev’s victory was widely expected, but not on this scale. This marks the collapse of the old system centred around GERB, but not necessarily a systemic transformation for Bulgaria,” explained Dimitar Keranov of the German Marshall Fund.

Speaking to his supporters after the exit polls were announced, Radev himself left little doubt as to how he viewed the significance of the moment.
“Progressive Bulgaria has won decisively. This is a victory of hope over mistrust. A victory of freedom over fear,” he declared.

The defeat of Boyko Borisov and his GERB party, which had dominated Bulgarian politics for years, is one of the defining features of this election night. The former prime minister, casting his vote in Sofia, reacted to the out
snippet: The parliamentary elections in Bulgaria delivered a result whose scale surprised even the most seasoned observers of the political scene in Sofia. Rumen Radev and his movement Progressive Bulgaria sec
creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: With Orbán gone, Magyar reshapes Slovak-Hungarian dynamics and V4 balance
content:
For years, contentious issues in once highly tense relations between Bratislava and Budapest were carefully kept under wraps. Now, with the Fico-Orbán tandem collapsing, the suppressed disputes may resurface as political landmines, experts think.

On 12 April, Hungarians ended Viktor Orbán’s 16-year rule in a record turnout, choosing Péter Magyar as his successor.

After his Tisza party “swept” the electoral map, Magyar used his victory speech to promise a reset: restoring Hungary’s reputation in Europe, unlocking EU funds, and creating conditions that would encourage Hungarians abroad to return home.

He also pledged to strengthen cooperation within the Visegrád Group (V4). Yet, when outlining his first foreign visits, he placed Poland firmly at the top of the list, without mentioning Slovakia or Czechia at all.

Several signs suggest that relations with Slovakia could at least cool. During the campaign, Magyar sharply criticized Slovakia’s amendment related to “the Beneš decrees” (1945), and has since reiterated that he intends to
snippet: For years, contentious issues in once highly tense relations between Bratislava and Budapest were carefully kept under wraps. Now, with the Fico-Orbán tandem collapsing, the suppressed disputes may re
creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: Loss of a Key Ally and Risk of Isolation: What Hungary’s Election Means for Czech Prime Minister Babiš
content:
Hungary’s parliamentary election on 12 April delivered a decisive victory to opposition leader Péter Magyar – a result that may have far-reaching consequences beyond Budapest. 

While Czech Prime Minister Andrej Babiš quickly congratulated the winner, his earlier statements made clear he had been rooting for incumbent Viktor Orbán, a close political ally. The outcome leaves Babiš facing a shifting geopolitical landscape in the European Union and the prospect of growing isolation within the European Council.

“Our programme is sovereignty and security, to fight illegal migration and to change the Green Deal,” Babiš declared on 30 June 2024 at a joint press conference with Orbán and Austrian far-right leader Herbert Kickl.

The trio had just launched a new political group in the European Parliament: Patriots for Europe. Coming shortly after the European elections, the alliance appeared to ride a wave of success for populist and far-right parties across the continent.

In those elections, Marine Le Pen’s party won in France, Babiš triumphed in Czec
